ladder.assisted: The agent triages the alert, reproduces the failure, finds the likely root cause, proposes the fix, and drafts the incident note.
ladder.autonomous: Known failure modes self-heal · the rest escalate with a full diagnosis and a postmortem already written.

jobs.replaces: The frantic hour of "what broke and why" every time an automation falls over · usually during a client deliverable.
jobs.notes: Reliability is a loop, not a heroic scramble · detect, triage, root-cause, fix, write it down so it can’t happen twice. The skill below runs that runbook so an outage costs minutes, not a morning.

jobs.name: Status Updates
jobs.desc: Clients see progress without asking for it.

ladder.manual: The client asks how it’s going; someone reconstructs the week from memory and Slack.
ladder.assisted: A weekly update drafts itself from the context file and shipped work · what moved, what’s next, what’s blocked · and the delivery lead edits and sends.
ladder.autonomous: Updates publish on schedule to email and the portal, generated from actual delivery activity, with the human only stepping in when there’s bad news to frame.

jobs.replaces: The update email that takes 30 minutes to write per client per week · or doesn’t get written, which is how clients conclude nothing is happening. Silence reads as stalling even when the work is flying.
jobs.notes: Cadence beats content: a short update every week outperforms a brilliant one monthly, because the rhythm itself is the reassurance. Generate from real activity · context files, commits, shipped deliverables · never from a human summarizing their week into a form, or the system dies in a fortnight. The trap: never let the automated update be the channel for bad news. Delays and problems get a human call first, the written update second.
jobs.human: Good news can ship on autopilot; bad news cannot. A human delivers every delay or problem personally before it appears in writing.

jobs.name: Meeting Follow-Ups
jobs.desc: Client call transcripts → action items → assigned and tracked.

ladder.manual: Whoever remembered, does it. Maybe.
ladder.assisted: Client transcripts → extracted action items, owners, deadlines · drafted into the recap that goes out same-day.
ladder.autonomous: Items assign themselves into the task system and chase their owners; the client recap sends on schedule.

jobs.replaces: Action items agreed on a client call that evaporate by Thursday · the quiet killer of delivery trust.
jobs.notes: Clients don’t churn over outcomes, they churn over feeling forgotten. Same-day recaps with named owners are the cheapest retention system ever built.
jobs.human: The delivery lead confirms owners and deadlines before the recap goes to the client · commitments are made by people.

jobs.name: Portal Sync
jobs.desc: Session summaries and deliverables flow into the client portal automatically.

ladder.manual: Someone remembers to update the portal after shipping. They do not remember.
ladder.assisted: End-of-session summaries and finished deliverables drafted into portal updates; a human approves what the client sees.
ladder.autonomous: Every working session writes its summary to the portal automatically · the client’s view and reality never drift apart.

jobs.replaces: The gap between work done and work visible. Without it, the portal decays into a brochure within two weeks and you’re back to manually updating two places · the work and the story about the work.
jobs.notes: This is the node that makes Portal Provisioning honest: the portal is only worth what it reflects, and manual updating is the failure mode that kills every client-facing dashboard ever built. Hook the sync to the natural end of work · session close, deliverable shipped, milestone hit · not to a separate update ritual. The trap is syncing raw internals: the client should see progress in their language, not your commit log.

jobs.name: Transcript Processing
jobs.desc: Every meeting becomes searchable, structured context.

ladder.manual: Someone re-listens to the recording to find that one thing the client said. They never do.
ladder.assisted: Every transcript processed into structure · decisions, action items, key facts, open questions · and filed against the right client.
ladder.autonomous: Transcripts flow from the notetaker into structured, searchable context automatically; every other agent on this map reads from the result.

jobs.replaces: Meetings that evaporate. Every client call contains decisions, commitments, and context worth money · and in most firms it lives nowhere but two people’s fading memory of a Tuesday.
jobs.notes: Call Capture records the meeting; this node makes it useful. Process into a consistent shape · decisions, commitments, facts learned, open threads · and file per client, because a searchable corpus of every client conversation is the raw material for half the Operations tree. The trap is storing raw transcripts and calling it done: an unstructured transcript is a haystack, and nobody queries haystacks.

jobs.name: Context Maintenance
jobs.desc: Per-client context files stay current as work happens.

ladder.manual: Context lives in one person’s head and a thousand Slack threads.
ladder.assisted: Per-client context files updated as work happens · calls, decisions, open items, current state.
ladder.autonomous: Every transcript, email, and deliverable updates the file automatically; any agent or human reads one page and is current.

jobs.replaces: The “wait, what’s the latest with this client?” tax every time anyone touches an account.
jobs.notes: This IS the knowledge base doctrine in practice · one living file per client that everything reads and writes. Start the convention before you automate it: the file format matters more than the tooling.

jobs.name: SOP Generation
jobs.desc: Turn delivered work into reusable playbooks.

ladder.manual: The process lives in the head of whoever did it last. Documentation is a thing you’ll do when things calm down.
ladder.assisted: After a project ships, the agent drafts the playbook from the actual work · context files, transcripts, what was built · and a human edits it into the canonical version.
ladder.autonomous: Repeated work patterns get flagged and drafted into SOPs automatically; the playbook library grows as a byproduct of delivery.

jobs.replaces: The expertise that walks out the door with whoever did the work. Every delivered project contains a repeatable playbook · and without this node, you rebuild it from scratch the next time, at full cost, forever.
jobs.notes: Write SOPs from evidence, not memory · the context files and transcripts record what actually happened, including the failure modes a human would forget to mention. The second delivery of any service type is the moment to extract the playbook: once is an event, twice is a pattern. The trap is writing aspirational SOPs describing how work should happen instead of how it demonstrably did · those get ignored by everyone, including their author.

jobs.name: Handoff Docs
jobs.desc: Clean documentation when a system ships to the client.

ladder.manual: The client gets a Loom recorded in one take and a promise to “ping me if anything breaks.”
ladder.assisted: Documentation drafted from the build itself · what it does, how to operate it, what to do when it misbehaves · and the builder reviews for accuracy before delivery.
ladder.autonomous: Docs generate and update alongside the build; by ship day the handoff pack already exists and matches what was actually delivered.

jobs.replaces: The post-handoff support tail: every undocumented system generates months of “quick questions” that are really unpaid retainer work. Bad handoffs also quietly cap your referrals · nobody recommends a system they can’t operate.
jobs.notes: Write for the person who will actually operate the system · usually a non-technical client-side owner, not a developer. Three layers cover it: what this is, how to use it day-to-day, and what to do when something looks wrong. Generate from the real build, not the proposal · scope drifted, and docs describing the system you planned rather than the one you shipped are worse than nothing. The trap is treating this as the last task: docs written in the final hour before handoff are the ones that generate the support tail.

jobs.name: Company Deep-Dive
jobs.desc: Financials, growth signals, org structure, strategic posture.

ladder.manual: Their website, their LinkedIn, your impression.
ladder.assisted: Financials, growth signals, org structure, and strategic posture compiled into a scored profile on demand.
ladder.autonomous: Target accounts re-profile on triggers · funding, leadership changes, hiring spikes · and alert the pipeline.

jobs.replaces: A junior analyst day ($300–500 equivalent) per company researched properly · so it only happens for deals already in motion, when it should happen before.
jobs.notes: The skill below scores fit, not just facts · the output is “should we pursue, and with what angle,” not a data dump.

jobs.name: Tech-Stack Detection
jobs.desc: What a target company runs, and where the gaps are.

ladder.manual: View-source on their homepage and a guess.
ladder.assisted: Stack detected per target · tools, platforms, recent migrations · with the gaps mapped against what you sell.
ladder.autonomous: Every account in the target list carries a live stack profile; a stack change that matches your wedge triggers an alert to the pipeline.

jobs.replaces: Pitching an integration to a company that can’t run it · or missing the account whose stack is screaming for what you sell. Manual stack-checking is 15–20 minutes per company across detection tools, job posts, and page source, so it happens for almost none of them.
jobs.notes: The gap is the product · “they run X without Y” is a campaign angle, not trivia. Cross-check detection tools against job postings and case studies; companies announce their stack every time they hire for it. Feeds Account Enrichment in Sales directly: enrichment is the bulk pass, this is the technical lens on it.

jobs.name: Funding & Financials Lookup
jobs.desc: Rounds, investors, revenue estimates, filings · who just got budget.

ladder.manual: Someone Googles "[company] funding" and guesses at revenue.
ladder.assisted: The agent pulls rounds, investors, revenue estimates, and recent filings into one clean read on a target’s buying power.
ladder.autonomous: Every account in the pipeline carries a current financial read · refreshed when something changes.

jobs.replaces: The 30-minute dig through Crunchbase, news, and filings to answer "do they actually have budget" · done for every account, by hand.
jobs.notes: The single best signal of "can they buy" is "did they just get money." This pulls funding, investors, and financial estimates so outreach lands where the budget actually is. Pairs with Account Monitoring to catch the raise the day it happens.

jobs.name: Buying-Committee Mapping
jobs.desc: Who actually decides · economic buyer, champion, blockers, their roles.

ladder.manual: You talk to one contact and hope they’re the decision-maker.
ladder.assisted: The agent maps the committee · economic buyer, champion, blockers, influencers · with each person’s role and likely concern.
ladder.autonomous: The map builds from the org and updates as new contacts enter the deal.

jobs.replaces: The deals that stall because you were selling to the wrong person · no map of who actually signs.
jobs.notes: Most B2B deals have 5–7 people involved and one of them quietly kills it. The skill below identifies the committee, their roles, and the concern each one needs answered.
jobs.human: A human runs the deal · this draws the board so they know who to win and in what order. Enterprise deals are lost to unmapped committees more than to bad pitches.

jobs.name: Person Research
jobs.desc: Background, content trail, interests, mutual ground for any contact.

ladder.manual: A LinkedIn skim.
ladder.assisted: Background, content trail, interests, and mutual ground compiled per person, with personalization hooks ranked.
ladder.autonomous: Every new contact in the CRM gets a profile automatically; briefs consume them.

jobs.replaces: Walking into rooms not knowing who you’re talking to.
jobs.notes: Same engine as Personalization Research in Sales · deployed here for relationships rather than sequences: partners, hires, investors, podcast guests.

jobs.name: Network Mapping
jobs.desc: Who knows whom · paths into a target account.

ladder.manual: Scrolling mutual connections and hoping a name rings a bell.
ladder.assisted: Paths into a target account mapped from your CRM, call history, and public graphs · ranked by strength, with a drafted intro ask per path.
ladder.autonomous: Every new target account arrives with its warm-path map attached; relationship data refreshes as calls and emails accumulate.

jobs.replaces: Cold outreach to accounts where a warm path already existed. A warm intro converts at multiples of cold · and most founders can’t name their second-degree reach into any given target.
jobs.notes: Your own data is the graph · CRM contacts, meeting attendees, email threads · long before any external tool. Build the convention of logging who-knows-whom in the knowledge base and the map compounds. Rank paths by real interaction recency, not connection count: a coffee last month beats a connection from 2019.
jobs.human: The map is AI’s; the ask is yours. Spending relationship capital · who to ask, for what, when · is judgment no agent should exercise, because the cost of a clumsy ask lands on a human relationship.

jobs.name: Warm-Path Finding
jobs.desc: The shortest real intro path into a target account · who can connect you.

ladder.manual: You cold-email an account you actually have two mutual connections into.
ladder.assisted: The agent finds the shortest real path into a target · who in your network can make the intro · and drafts the ask.
ladder.autonomous: Every target account is checked for a warm path before any cold outreach is attempted.

jobs.replaces: The cold open you didn’t need to send · a warm intro path existed and nobody looked for it.
jobs.notes: A warm intro converts many times better than a cold one and most teams never check whether they have one. This finds the path and writes the intro ask · the difference between "who are you" and "happy to chat, [mutual] speaks highly".

jobs.name: Competitor Teardown
jobs.desc: Pricing, positioning, strengths, weaknesses, exploitable gaps.

ladder.manual: You visited their pricing page once.
ladder.assisted: Full teardown on demand: pricing, positioning, strengths, weaknesses, exploitable gaps · cited.
ladder.autonomous: Tracked competitors re-tear quarterly; material changes (pricing, packaging, positioning) trigger alerts.

jobs.replaces: Competing on guesswork. Most firms can’t articulate a competitor’s pricing or positioning beyond adjectives.
jobs.notes: The exploitable-gaps section is the deliverable · everything else is context. The skill below structures the attack: where they’re weak, where you’re strong, what to say in-market.

jobs.name: Vertical Analysis
jobs.desc: Is this vertical worth entering · demand, budgets, buying behavior.

ladder.manual: One client in the vertical went well, so the vertical must be good.
ladder.assisted: Demand signals, budget reality, buying behavior, and competitive density compiled into a scored go/no-go per vertical.
ladder.autonomous: A watchlist of candidate verticals re-scores on a schedule; a vertical crossing the threshold surfaces with the entry angle attached.

jobs.replaces: Entering a vertical on one good anecdote · then discovering six months in that the budgets were never there. A proper market assessment runs $5–15k from a research firm, so most firms skip it and pay in wasted quarters instead.
jobs.notes: Score verticals on pain frequency, budget authority, and reachability · not market size. A small vertical that buys fast beats a huge one that deliberates. The output should end in a decision and an entry play, not a landscape summary; if the verdict is go, it feeds ICP Definition in Sales as the next node.

jobs.name: Pricing Research
jobs.desc: What the market charges, anchors, and packaging patterns.

ladder.manual: You checked what one competitor charges and went slightly under.
ladder.assisted: Market rates, anchor points, and packaging patterns compiled per offer · what the market charges, how it bundles, where the ceiling actually sits.
ladder.autonomous: Tracked competitors and market sources re-sweep on a schedule; pricing moves and packaging changes surface before your next proposal goes out.

jobs.replaces: Pricing by mimicry or by nerve. Most firms set prices once, from two competitor pages and a feeling, and leave 20–40% of margin on the table for years.
jobs.notes: Packaging patterns matter more than price points · how the market structures tiers, retainers, and anchors tells you how buyers expect to buy. Mine your own closed-won and closed-lost notes too: what prospects compared you against is pricing data nobody external can sell you. The trap is averaging · price against the segment you want, not the whole market.

jobs.name: TAM / Market Sizing
jobs.desc: How many companies actually fit · the real size of the prize.

ladder.manual: Someone multiplies two guesses and calls it the TAM.
ladder.assisted: The agent counts the companies that actually fit the ICP in a market, by segment, with the assumptions shown.
ladder.autonomous: Market size recalculates as the ICP sharpens and new segments are tested.

jobs.replaces: The board-deck number pulled from thin air · or a $15k analyst engagement to size a market.
jobs.notes: A real TAM is a count of qualifying companies, not a percentage of a McKinsey headline. The skill below sizes the prize bottom-up · how many real targets exist · so you know whether a vertical is worth the campaign before you build it.

jobs.name: Account Monitoring
jobs.desc: Watch a target list for funding, hiring, leadership, and tech changes · ongoing.

ladder.manual: You re-research an account from scratch every time you remember it exists.
ladder.assisted: The agent watches a target list for funding, hiring, leadership, and tech changes and surfaces what moved.
ladder.autonomous: The watchlist runs continuously · every meaningful change on a target account lands as a scored signal.

jobs.replaces: Finding out a target raised, hired a VP, or switched tools weeks late · from their newsletter, not your radar.
jobs.notes: Timing beats personalization. The skill below watches your target accounts so the trigger event · the raise, the new hire, the migration · reaches you the morning it happens, while the window is open. The first job of the Monitoring function.

jobs.name: News & Mention Tracking
jobs.desc: Every mention of your brand, competitors, and topics · as it lands.

ladder.manual: You find out you were mentioned when someone screenshots it to you.
ladder.assisted: The agent tracks every mention of your brand, competitors, and key topics across news and social as it lands.
ladder.autonomous: Mentions are tracked, classified by sentiment and importance, and the ones that matter get routed.

jobs.replaces: A $200/mo media-monitoring tool, plus the gap it leaves on competitor and topic coverage.
jobs.notes: You can’t respond to what you can’t see · a bad review, a competitor’s launch, a journalist’s question all have short fuses. This watches the conversation so you’re early, not surprised.

jobs.name: Alert Routing
jobs.desc: The signals that matter reach the right person · the noise gets filtered.

ladder.manual: Every signal pings the same channel and everyone tunes it out.
ladder.assisted: The agent scores each signal for relevance and urgency and routes only what matters to the right person or channel.
ladder.autonomous: Signals self-triage · high-value ones reach the right human with context, the rest log silently.

jobs.replaces: The monitoring that becomes noise · 200 alerts a day, all ignored within a week.
jobs.notes: Monitoring without routing is just a louder inbox. The skill below scores and routes · the Series B of a core account goes to the founder with the intro path attached; the rest stays out of the way. This is what makes monitoring usable instead of exhausting.

jobs.name: Research Reports
jobs.desc: Any question → structured, cited report with diagrams.

ladder.manual: Twenty open tabs and a hunch.
ladder.assisted: Any question → structured, cited report with diagrams, in an hour instead of a week.
ladder.autonomous: Standing questions (market shifts, vertical signals) refresh on schedule and publish to the knowledge base.

jobs.replaces: $2–5k boutique research engagements, or the decision made without one.
jobs.notes: Pair with Adversarial Verification before anything load-bearing: claims get attacked before they get believed.

jobs.name: Data Visualization
jobs.desc: Turn a pile of research into charts and diagrams that explain themselves.

ladder.manual: Findings get pasted into a doc as paragraphs and bullet points.
ladder.assisted: The agent turns the research into charts and diagrams that carry the point at a glance.
ladder.autonomous: Every report ships with the right visual auto-generated from its data.

jobs.replaces: The research that lands as a wall of text nobody reads · or the analyst who spends an afternoon making one chart.
jobs.notes: A finding that needs three paragraphs to land is a finding that won’t. The skill below renders research into the chart or diagram that makes it obvious · the visual layer on top of Research Reports.

jobs.name: Adversarial Verification
jobs.desc: Claims get attacked before they get believed.

ladder.manual: It sounded right and the source looked legit.
ladder.assisted: Every load-bearing claim gets attacked before it ships · counter-evidence hunted, sources checked for independence, confidence graded per claim.
ladder.autonomous: Verification runs as a gate in the research pipeline: nothing publishes to the knowledge base above a stated confidence without surviving the attack pass.

jobs.replaces: The confident wrong number that makes it into the proposal, the deck, or the deal. One bad claim repeated to a client costs more credibility than a hundred verified ones earn.
jobs.notes: Run it as a separate pass with a separate instruction · “find why this is wrong” · never as a self-check by the agent that wrote the claim; the author defending its own work is how errors survive. Grade claims, don’t just flag them: verified, plausible, contested, wrong. The trap is verifying everything · gate only what’s load-bearing, or the pipeline slows to the speed of paranoia.
jobs.human: A human owns what ships under the company’s name. Verification reduces the risk; it never transfers the accountability.

jobs.name: Ticket Triage
jobs.desc: Classify, prioritize, and route every support request the moment it lands.

ladder.manual: One shared inbox, oldest unread first, urgency optional.
ladder.assisted: Every request auto-tagged · topic, urgency, sentiment, owner · the moment it lands; a human confirms the routing on the gnarly ones.
ladder.autonomous: Tickets route themselves to the right queue with context attached; priority reorders live as sentiment and SLA clocks change.

jobs.replaces: The first hour of every support shift · a rep reading, labeling, and forwarding before anyone gets helped. Misrouted tickets bounce between owners 2–3 times and add a day per bounce.
jobs.notes: Triage is the front door for the whole Support tree · Escalations and FAQ & Self-Serve both read its tags, so get the categories right before you automate the routing. Start with 5–7 categories from your last 200 tickets, not 30 invented ones. The trap: optimizing routing speed while the labels are wrong · bad tags at high speed just misroute faster.

jobs.name: FAQ & Self-Serve
jobs.desc: Answer the questions that repeat · and turn them into living help content.

ladder.manual: Copy-paste from a doc nobody updates.
ladder.assisted: Repeat questions answered from a living knowledge base; new repeats flagged and drafted into help content.
ladder.autonomous: The help corpus grows itself · every novel resolved ticket becomes a draft article; deflection rate tracked.

jobs.replaces: The 40–60% of support volume that is the same fifteen questions, answered by hand, forever.
jobs.notes: The flywheel is the point: support tickets are your help-content roadmap, ranked by frequency. Deflection compounds · every article works every day after it ships.
jobs.human: A human reviews new auto-drafted articles before they publish, and owns the escalation line the moment an answer isn’t in the corpus.

jobs.name: Escalations
jobs.desc: Spot the angry, the churn-risk, and the edge case · and get a human there fast.

ladder.manual: A rep forwards the scary email with “can you look at this?”
ladder.assisted: Sentiment spikes, churn signals, and repeat-contact patterns flag in real time; a human gets pinged with the full thread history and a suggested first move.
ladder.autonomous: Detection runs across every channel; flagged accounts page the right owner within minutes, briefed · and the system tracks whether the save worked.

jobs.replaces: The churn you find out about in a one-star review. By the time a frustrated customer asks for a manager, they’ve usually been simmering across three polite tickets nobody connected.
jobs.notes: Tune for false positives over false negatives · an unnecessary human glance costs two minutes, a missed escalation costs the account. The detection layer should read across tickets, not within them: three mildly annoyed messages in two weeks is a louder signal than one heated one. Wire flagged accounts into Health Scoring so the risk shows up on the account, not just the ticket.
jobs.human: An angry customer never gets a bot. The system’s entire job is detection and speed · the conversation that follows is a human’s, with full context in hand.

jobs.name: Macro Authoring
jobs.desc: Turn the tickets that repeat into canned, on-brand responses agents reuse.

ladder.manual: Support reps wing the same fifty answers a hundred different ways.
ladder.assisted: The agent reads the recurring tickets and drafts canned, on-brand macros for the team to reuse.
ladder.autonomous: Macros generate and update themselves as new repeat questions emerge · the library stays current without a sweep.

jobs.replaces: Every agent re-typing the same answer from scratch · and answering it slightly differently each time.
jobs.notes: The fastest way to cut response time is to stop writing the same answer twice. This mines the ticket history for what repeats and writes the reusable, on-voice response · feeding the same source that powers FAQ & Self-Serve.

jobs.name: Onboarding Journeys
jobs.desc: Guide every new customer through activation, step by step.

ladder.manual: A welcome email, a PDF, and hope they figure it out.
ladder.assisted: Step-by-step activation sequences drafted per customer segment; nudges trigger on stalled milestones, human sends the rescue note.
ladder.autonomous: Every new customer walks a journey that adapts to their progress · stuck on step two gets help for step two, not a generic check-in. Completion rates tracked per step.

jobs.replaces: 2–4 CSM hours per new account sending the same welcome sequence by hand · or the silent version, where customers who stall in week one become the churn stat of month three.
jobs.notes: Define activation first · the one action that predicts retention · and build the journey backwards from it. Instrument every step so you can see exactly where customers stall; that drop-off chart is your product roadmap. The trap: a beautiful 12-email sequence nobody measured. Five steps with completion data beats twelve with vibes.

jobs.name: Health Scoring
jobs.desc: Score every account on usage, sentiment, and engagement · before renewal season.

ladder.manual: Renewal panic, quarterly.
ladder.assisted: Accounts scored on usage, sentiment, and engagement; the at-risk list surfaces monthly with reasons.
ladder.autonomous: Scores update continuously; threshold crossings trigger plays · outreach, check-in, escalation · before renewal season.

jobs.replaces: Finding out an account was at risk in the cancellation email.
jobs.notes: Sentiment from call transcripts and support threads is the leading indicator usage data misses · accounts go quiet before they go.
jobs.human: The score surfaces risk; a human makes the call. No automated message ever goes to an at-risk account without an owner deciding to send it.

jobs.name: Churn Prediction
jobs.desc: Predict which accounts are about to slip · before the usage even dips.

ladder.manual: You find out an account churned when the cancellation comes through.
ladder.assisted: The agent models which accounts are likely to slip · weighting usage trend, sentiment, and engagement · before the obvious dip.
ladder.autonomous: At-risk accounts are flagged early with the reason and a recommended play, continuously.

jobs.replaces: The renewal that surprises you by not happening · the exit interview that should’ve been a save three weeks earlier.
jobs.notes: Health Scoring tells you the current state; this predicts the trajectory. It catches the leading indicators · the champion who went quiet, the login cadence softening · while a conversation can still turn it around.

jobs.name: Renewals & Expansion
jobs.desc: Time renewal conversations and spot upsell openings.

ladder.manual: A calendar reminder set at signing, if anyone remembered.
ladder.assisted: Renewals surface 90 days out with health context and a drafted opener; usage patterns flag accounts ready for the expansion conversation.
ladder.autonomous: Every account carries a renewal runway and an expansion readiness signal; the owner gets the play, the timing, and the talking points · and decides whether to run it.

jobs.replaces: Renewals discovered two weeks out and handled in a panic, plus the expansion revenue nobody asked for because nobody noticed the account had outgrown its plan.
jobs.notes: The 90-day rule is the whole game: a renewal conversation started early is a planning session, started late it’s a negotiation. Expansion signals live in usage data · seats maxed, limits hit, new teams appearing · so wire this to whatever Health Scoring reads. The trap: leading the renewal call with price. Lead with the value ledger · what they got this year · and let the number follow.
jobs.human: Renewal and upsell conversations are human, full stop. AI decides when and arms the talker · timing, account history, the expansion angle. The ask itself never automates.

jobs.name: Advocacy & Referrals
jobs.desc: Turn your happiest customers into referrals, reviews, and case studies.

ladder.manual: You mean to ask your best client for a referral; you never do.
ladder.assisted: The agent spots accounts at peak happiness and drafts the ask · referral, review, or case study · at the right moment.
ladder.autonomous: Advocacy triggers off health peaks · the right ask goes out when satisfaction is highest, tracked through to the asset.

jobs.replaces: The case studies and referrals you never ask for · happy clients who would gladly help if anyone caught the moment.
jobs.notes: Your happiest customers are your cheapest growth channel and the one nobody works systematically. This watches for the high-water mark · a win, a glowing reply, a renewal · and makes the ask while the feeling’s fresh.

jobs.name: QBR Prep
jobs.desc: Build the quarterly business review from real account data.

ladder.manual: Last quarter’s deck, new dates, same screenshots.
ladder.assisted: Account data, usage trends, support history, and open items compiled into the house QBR template; the CSM edits the narrative and the asks.
ladder.autonomous: Decks generate on the quarterly calendar automatically · wins quantified, risks flagged, next-quarter recommendations drafted · ready for a human polish pass.

jobs.replaces: 3–5 CSM hours per account per quarter assembling slides from scattered data · which is why most QBRs are either skipped or improvised from memory in the parking lot.
jobs.notes: A QBR is a renewal argument delivered early · every slide should answer “why keep paying us.” Quantify outcomes in the customer’s units (hours saved, tickets deflected, revenue touched), not your feature usage. The trap: a data dump with no point of view. The recommendations slide is the deliverable; everything before it is evidence.

jobs.name: Engagement & Replies
jobs.desc: Every member question and mention gets a response in the house voice.

ladder.manual: The founder replies in bursts at midnight, then goes silent for a week.
ladder.assisted: Every question and mention gets a drafted reply in the house voice within the hour; a human approves and posts.
ladder.autonomous: Routine questions answered directly from the knowledge base; novel or sensitive threads route to a human with a draft attached. Response time tracked like an SLA.

jobs.replaces: A community manager’s core hours · or the slow death where member questions sit unanswered for two days and the space quietly becomes a noticeboard.
jobs.notes: Response speed is the heartbeat of a community · a question answered in 30 minutes teaches members to ask more, one answered in three days teaches them to stop. The voice file is the prerequisite: an off-tone reply in a community is worse than silence, because members can tell. The trap: letting the agent answer everything · the founder showing up personally on the threads that matter is the product.

jobs.name: Moderation
jobs.desc: Keep the space clean · spam, conduct, and noise handled quietly.

ladder.manual: Someone notices the spam after twelve members already have.
ladder.assisted: Spam, self-promo, and conduct flags caught on posting and queued with context; a human approves removals and warnings.
ladder.autonomous: Obvious spam removed silently on sight; borderline conduct cases land in a review queue with the member’s history attached and a recommended action.

jobs.replaces: The founder deleting crypto spam at 7am, and the slower cost: one unchecked bad actor or pile-on thread can empty a community faster than any feature can fill it.
jobs.notes: Write the rules before you build the enforcer · moderation without a published code of conduct reads as arbitrary, and arbitrary kills trust faster than spam does. Tier the response: silent removal for bots, private note for first-time line-crossers, human conversation for members. The trap: over-moderating early. A quiet community needs friction removed, not added.
jobs.human: Spam removal automates clean. Conduct calls don’t · warning or removing a real member is a judgment a human makes, with the receipts the system gathered.

jobs.name: Member Spotlights
jobs.desc: Find and celebrate member wins · the retention engine.

ladder.manual: A shout-out when the founder happens to see something good.
ladder.assisted: Wins, milestones, and standout posts surfaced weekly from community activity; spotlight drafts ready in the house voice for a human to personalize and post.
ladder.autonomous: A standing spotlight cadence runs itself · candidates ranked by story strength, drafts queued, posted on schedule with a human glance.

jobs.replaces: The retention engine nobody runs because it’s nobody’s job: member wins scroll past unnoticed, and the people most likely to renew never get told they matter.
jobs.notes: A spotlighted member renews, posts more, and recruits · it’s the cheapest retention play in the tree. Ask permission before celebrating anyone publicly; a surprise spotlight on a private win burns trust. The trap: spotlighting only the whales. Celebrating a small win from a quiet member moves the whole room; celebrating the same three power users is just a leaderboard.

jobs.name: Event Coordination
jobs.desc: Run the AMAs, calls, and spotlights on a cadence the community can count on.

ladder.manual: Events happen sporadically when someone remembers to organise one.
ladder.assisted: The agent schedules the cadence, drafts the promo, sends reminders, and preps the run-of-show for each session.
ladder.autonomous: The event calendar runs on rails · scheduled, promoted, reminded, and recapped, with the human just showing up to host.

jobs.replaces: The community that goes quiet because the AMAs and calls stopped happening · running events is a part-time job nobody owns.
jobs.notes: A community lives on rhythm · members stay for the next thing on the calendar. This runs the AMAs, calls, and spotlights on a dependable cadence so the room always has a reason to come back.

jobs.name: Invoice Generation
jobs.desc: Branded invoices per entity, currency, and tax treatment.

ladder.manual: A template, edited by hand, eventually sent.
ladder.assisted: Branded invoices generated per entity, currency, and tax treatment from the deal record.
ladder.autonomous: Milestones and retainer schedules trigger invoices automatically; numbering, tax, and filing handled.

jobs.replaces: The founder doing invoices on Sunday night, occasionally wrong, across entities and currencies.
jobs.notes: Encode the entity routing once · which company invoices which client, in what currency, with what tax line · and a recurring error class disappears permanently.
jobs.human: A human glances at every invoice over a threshold before it sends. Money out the door wrong costs more than the glance.

jobs.name: Payment Tracking
jobs.desc: Know what’s paid, due, and late · without opening a bank app.

ladder.manual: You check the bank when you have a bad feeling.
ladder.assisted: Every invoice tracked against incoming payments · paid, due, late · in one view, updated daily, currencies normalized.
ladder.autonomous: Payments reconcile against invoices automatically; late ones hand themselves to Collections, and the cash position is always current without anyone asking.

jobs.replaces: The weekly bank-app archaeology session · logging into three accounts across two currencies to reconstruct who’s paid. Plus the invoice you forgot was unpaid until the quarter closed.
jobs.notes: Pull from the source of truth · bank feeds or the payment processor · not from memory or the invoice tool’s optimistic status field. Normalize multi-currency to one reporting currency at a consistent rate convention, or every downstream number argues with itself. The trap: tracking what you sent instead of what landed. An invoice is a hope; a deposit is a fact.

jobs.name: Collections
jobs.desc: Overdue invoices get chased politely and persistently.

ladder.manual: You notice an unpaid invoice during a low moment.
ladder.assisted: Overdue invoices get drafted nudges · polite, persistent, escalating on schedule · queued for your approval.
ladder.autonomous: The sequence runs itself: day-3 nudge, day-10 firmer, day-21 escalation, with payment status checked before every send.

jobs.replaces: The awkwardness of chasing money · which is why founders chase late and lose weeks of cash flow to their own politeness.
jobs.notes: An agent is never embarrassed to ask. Tone-match the relationship · the sequence should sound like your best self on a confident day, every time.
jobs.human: Tone escalation past the second nudge gets a human decision · the relationship is worth more than the invoice.

jobs.name: Revenue Reporting
jobs.desc: Monthly revenue truth across entities and currencies.

ladder.manual: A spreadsheet with a tab per month and a formula someone broke in March.
ladder.assisted: Monthly revenue compiled across entities and currencies · by client, by type, recurring vs. one-off · drafted for a human sanity check.
ladder.autonomous: The report generates itself on the first of the month from reconciled payment data; anomalies get flagged, not buried.

jobs.replaces: Either $200–500/month of bookkeeper time assembling a report that arrives three weeks stale, or the founder rebuilding the same spreadsheet every month and trusting it less each time.
jobs.notes: One number per question: what came in, from whom, through which entity, recurring or not. Split recurring from project revenue from day one · it’s the split every future decision (and buyer) cares about. The trap is reporting invoiced revenue as received revenue; build only on reconciled payments or the report is fiction with formatting.

jobs.name: Goal Pacing
jobs.desc: Tracking against the income target, with pace forecasts.

ladder.manual: A number in your head and a feeling about it.
ladder.assisted: Live pace against target · monthly and annual · with a forecast of where the current trajectory lands.
ladder.autonomous: Pace alerts with diagnosis: which pipeline gap, which retainer change, which seasonality is moving the landing point.

jobs.replaces: Discovering in November that the year’s number was never going to happen.
jobs.notes: The forecast matters more than the scoreboard · “you finish at 71% on current pace” in June is actionable; the same number in December is an obituary.

jobs.name: Cash-Flow Forecasting
jobs.desc: Project cash in and out · know your runway before it becomes an emergency.

ladder.manual: Cash gets checked by looking at the bank balance and feeling either fine or scared.
ladder.assisted: The agent projects inflows and outflows, computes runway, and builds a 3/6/12-month forward view with the assumptions shown.
ladder.autonomous: The forecast updates as money moves · runway and scenario models refresh themselves and flag when a threshold is crossed.

jobs.replaces: The fractional CFO’s headline deliverable · or, more often, no forecast at all and a founder guessing at runway.
jobs.notes: Revenue Reporting tells you what happened; this tells you what’s coming. The skill below projects cash in and out, calculates runway, and runs scenarios ("hire two", "lose the biggest client", "double ad spend") so "can we afford this" has an answer before it’s urgent.
jobs.human: A human owns the assumptions and the calls that follow · this does the math, fast and honestly, and shows its work. The decisions stay human.

jobs.name: Budget Tracking
jobs.desc: Spend vs budget by category, with overruns flagged early.

ladder.manual: Spend is reconciled against budget quarterly, if ever.
ladder.assisted: The agent tracks actual spend against budget by category and flags overruns as they emerge.
ladder.autonomous: Budget variance is monitored continuously · a category trending over gets flagged mid-month, not after.

jobs.replaces: The budget set once in January and never looked at again · overruns discovered at tax time.
jobs.notes: A budget you don’t check is a wish. This watches spend against plan by category so the overrun is a flag you act on, not a number you explain after the fact. Pairs with Cash-Flow Forecasting as the Finance Ops function.

jobs.name: CRM Sync
jobs.desc: Deals, contacts, and statuses stay current everywhere.

ladder.manual: The CRM gets updated in a guilty batch before someone looks at it.
ladder.assisted: Deals, contacts, and statuses sync across systems · CRM, invoicing, project tracker · with changes drafted from real activity for approval.
ladder.autonomous: Calls, emails, and payments update records as they happen; every system agrees, and nobody types the same client name twice.

jobs.replaces: The gap between what the CRM says and what’s actually true · which quietly invalidates every report, forecast, and reactivation play built on top of it.
jobs.notes: Pick one system of record per object · deals live here, money lives there · and make everything else a mirror, never a second author. Sync from activity (transcripts, payments, sent emails), not from human discipline, because human discipline is what failed in the first place. The trap: two-way sync between two systems that both think they’re the boss.
jobs.human: Merges and deletions get a human click. A bad sync that creates a duplicate is annoying; one that destroys a record is archaeology.

jobs.name: Expense Categorization
jobs.desc: Spend categorized for the accountant before they ask.

ladder.manual: A folder called “receipts” and a strong belief that future-you will deal with it.
ladder.assisted: Transactions pulled from feeds and auto-categorized to your chart of accounts, with the uncertain tail queued for a 10-minute weekly review.
ladder.autonomous: Spend lands pre-categorized per entity with receipts attached; the accountant gets a clean file, not a quiz.

jobs.replaces: $150–400/month of bookkeeper categorization, or the annual shoebox week where the founder relives twelve months of receipts to answer the accountant’s spreadsheet.
jobs.notes: Get the chart of accounts from your accountant first and categorize into it · inventing your own categories just creates a translation job later. Auto-accept the recurring 80% (same vendor, same category, every month) and only surface the new and ambiguous. The trap is multi-entity spend on one card: encode which entity owns which subscription once, or every month leaks misattributed costs.
jobs.human: Tax treatment is judgment, not classification · deductibility calls and anything the accountant would raise an eyebrow at stay human.

jobs.name: Document Filing & Retrieval
jobs.desc: Every document named, filed, and findable in ten seconds.

ladder.manual: Files live in seven places with names like "final_v3_FINAL".
ladder.assisted: The agent names, files, and indexes every document so any of them is findable in seconds.
ladder.autonomous: Documents are filed and tagged on arrival, with retention rules applied · nothing is ever lost or kept too long.

jobs.replaces: The Friday afternoon spent hunting for "the signed SOW from March" · multiplied across everyone, every week.
jobs.notes: "Where’s that document" is a tax every business pays daily. The skill below enforces naming, filing, and an index so retrieval is ten seconds, not a search party · and applies retention so old records don’t pile up.

jobs.name: Contract Lifecycle
jobs.desc: Renewals, expiries, and obligations tracked so nothing sneaks up.

ladder.manual: Renewal and expiry dates live in someone’s memory and a forgotten calendar invite.
ladder.assisted: The agent tracks every contract’s renewal, expiry, and obligations and surfaces them well before the deadline.
ladder.autonomous: The lifecycle runs itself · renewals flagged in time to act, obligations tracked, nothing sneaks up.

jobs.replaces: The auto-renewal you forgot to cancel · the client contract that lapsed because nobody tracked the date.
jobs.notes: Contracts have dates that cost money when missed · a lapsed client agreement, a vendor auto-renewing at a worse rate. This tracks every obligation and deadline so the calendar works for you instead of against you.

jobs.name: Entity Compliance
jobs.desc: Filings and obligations per entity, tracked and scheduled.

ladder.manual: The accountant emails you something urgent and you learn a deadline existed.
ladder.assisted: Every entity’s obligations · filings, renewals, tax dates, license deadlines · tracked in one calendar with lead-time alerts and prepared checklists.
ladder.autonomous: The system watches the obligations, drafts the routine paperwork, and escalates with everything assembled; humans sign and submit.

jobs.replaces: Missed filings, and what they cost: late penalties, a suspended entity, or a frozen bank account discovered the week you need it. Multi-entity founders carry a deadline list nobody actually maintains.
jobs.notes: Build the obligation register per entity first · jurisdiction, filing, frequency, deadline, who files it · straight from your accountant and registered agent, not from memory. Alerts at 30, 14, and 3 days, with the longest lead on anything requiring a third party. The trap: tracking only tax. Trade licenses, registered agents, visa renewals, and substance requirements kill entities just as dead.
jobs.human: Filings get signed by a human, full stop. AI tracks, prepares, and nags · it never submits anything with legal weight on its own.

jobs.name: Calendar Management
jobs.desc: Scheduling, conflicts, buffers, and timezone math handled.

ladder.manual: You are the scheduling link, and the link is tired.
ladder.assisted: Conflicts caught, buffers enforced, timezones handled; reschedule requests come back with sane options instead of a shrug.
ladder.autonomous: The calendar defends itself · focus blocks protected, travel and prep time auto-added, low-priority requests deflected to the right slot without a human in the loop.

jobs.replaces: A part-time EA’s core duty ($15–25k/year), or the founder doing timezone math at midnight and the double-booking that torches a first impression.
jobs.notes: Write the rules before the automation: meeting hours, buffer lengths, which meeting types deserve which slots, what’s sacred. The agent enforces policy · if there’s no policy, it just automates chaos faster. The trap is optimizing for openness: a calendar that accepts everything efficiently is a calendar that destroys deep work efficiently.

jobs.name: Email Triage
jobs.desc: The inbox sorted by what actually needs the human.

ladder.manual: Inbox open all day, attention rented out one notification at a time.
ladder.assisted: Every email sorted on arrival · needs-you / FYI / automated noise · with drafted replies queued on the routine ones.
ladder.autonomous: The inbox becomes a briefing: the handful that genuinely need a human, surfaced with context and a draft; the rest filed, answered, or archived by standing rules.

jobs.replaces: The 1–2 hours a day a founder spends in the inbox, of which maybe fifteen minutes is decisions and the rest is sorting other people’s priorities.
jobs.notes: The classifier needs your context to know what matters · a client name, an open deal, an overdue invoice all change an email’s priority, which is why this reads from the knowledge base. Start with triage-only for two weeks and audit the calls before letting it draft. The trap is over-filtering: one important email in spam-purgatory costs more trust than a hundred correctly filed newsletters earn.
jobs.human: Triage is autonomous; sending is not. Replies go out after a human approves · and the agent never deletes, only archives, so a misjudged email is a search away, not gone.

jobs.name: Candidate Sourcing
jobs.desc: Find and rank candidates for open roles across platforms.

ladder.manual: Post the job, pray, hire the least-bad applicant in week six.
ladder.assisted: A real role scorecard drives searches across platforms; candidates come back ranked against it with reasons, not vibes.
ladder.autonomous: Open roles run standing searches; new matches land scored and deduped, outreach drafted, pipeline always warm before the seat is empty.

jobs.replaces: A recruiter’s 20–25% placement fee, or 10+ founder-hours per role scrolling profiles · which is why most small companies hire from whoever happened to apply.
jobs.notes: The scorecard is the whole build: outcomes the role must deliver, must-have evidence, disqualifiers · written before the first search, stored where agents read it. Rank on demonstrated work over keyword density; titles lie, portfolios don’t. The trap is sourcing for the résumé you’d recognize instead of the outcome you need · the scorecard exists to stop that.

jobs.name: Screening & Scheduling
jobs.desc: First-pass screens and interview logistics, handled.

ladder.manual: You skim forty CVs on a Sunday and interview whoever annoyed you least.
ladder.assisted: Every applicant screened against the scorecard · structured questions asked and scored · with a shortlist and reasoning for human review; interviews book themselves.
ladder.autonomous: Screens run on arrival, the calendar fills with only above-bar candidates, and everyone else gets a prompt, decent no · drafted by the system, signed off by a human.

jobs.replaces: The 30 minutes per applicant a first-pass screen actually takes, multiplied by the 80% who were never going to make it · plus the scheduling ping-pong that loses good candidates to faster companies.
jobs.notes: Structured beats freeform: same questions, same rubric, every candidate · it’s fairer and it makes the AI scoring actually comparable. Speed is a weapon here; the best candidates are gone in days, and same-day screening plus instant booking wins talent that slower companies never even meet. The trap: optimizing for interview polish. Score evidence of doing the job, not fluency in describing it.
jobs.human: AI filters; humans reject and advance. Every no on a real candidate is a human call · a person’s livelihood deserves more than a threshold, and so does your reputation in a small market.

jobs.name: Onboarding & Training
jobs.desc: New hires get the playbooks, access, and 30-day plan automatically.

ladder.manual: Day one is a laptop, a Slack invite, and “shadow Sarah for a bit.”
ladder.assisted: Offer accepted → access checklist, playbooks, tool walkthroughs, and a 30-day plan generated from the role scorecard; a human assigns the mentor and the first real task.
ladder.autonomous: The whole arc runs itself · accounts provisioned, training sequenced, check-ins scheduled, progress tracked · and the manager gets a flag only when someone’s off pace.

jobs.replaces: The new hire’s lost first month · asking around for access, absorbing context by osmosis · and the senior person’s week spent re-explaining what should have been written down.
jobs.notes: This node is only as good as the knowledge base behind it · onboarding is really just the company’s documented self, sequenced into 30 days. Write the plan as outcomes per week (what they can do unsupervised) rather than content consumed. The trap is information-dump onboarding: forty documents in week one teaches nothing; one real task with the relevant playbook attached teaches everything.

jobs.name: HR & Policy Assistant
jobs.desc: Answer staff policy questions from the real handbook · leave, expenses, onboarding admin.

ladder.manual: Policy questions interrupt whoever’s nearest, answered from memory and inconsistently.
ladder.assisted: The agent answers staff questions from the real handbook · leave, expenses, process · with a citation, and handles onboarding admin.
ladder.autonomous: Routine people-ops runs itself · policy answered from source, new-hire admin sequenced, the handbook kept current.

jobs.replaces: The first HR hire a small team can’t justify yet · and the founder fielding "how much leave do I have" between sales calls.
jobs.notes: The skill below is grounded strictly in the company’s own policy docs · it never guesses a policy, it cites the source, and it escalates the hard stuff. It removes the founder as the help desk for "what’s our policy on…".
jobs.human: Anything legal, sensitive, or about comp/termination escalates to a human immediately · this answers the routine 80% from documented policy and never invents one.

jobs.name: Hiring Decisions
jobs.desc: AI can source and screen, but the actual call on who joins the team is a founder's read on character and fit that stays human.

jobs.name: Compliance Sign-Off
jobs.desc: Final responsibility for legal filings and entity obligations carries personal liability, so a human signs off even when the calendar is automated.

jobs.name: Banking Relationships
jobs.desc: Opening accounts, negotiating terms, and the trust with banks and the accountant are relationship calls a person owns.

jobs.name: Spend Authority
jobs.desc: What the business actually spends money on, and where to cut, is a strategic taste call that the founder keeps despite full visibility being automated.

jobs.name: Renewal Negotiation
jobs.desc: The actual renewal and upsell conversation is a relationship and a deal, not a workflow. AI can time and tee it up, but a person owns the price, the terms, and the trust.

jobs.name: Strategic Accounts
jobs.desc: The handful of accounts that define the business need a named human owner who knows their politics and roadmap · these relationships are the moat, not a queue item.

jobs.name: Community Voice
jobs.desc: The personality, culture, and judgment calls that make a community feel alive can't be templated · a human sets the tone and decides which moments to celebrate.

jobs.name: Save Calls
jobs.desc: When an account is genuinely about to churn, the rescue is a human-to-human conversation · AI surfaces the risk, but a person makes the save.

jobs.name: Closing the Deal
jobs.desc: The actual ask, the room read, and the final yes stay with a person. AI preps the artifacts and the brief, but trust is transferred human to human.

jobs.name: Discounting & Concessions
jobs.desc: What to give away to win a deal is a strategic call with margin and precedent at stake. The model can suggest anchors, but the concession is the founder's to make.

jobs.name: Strategic Account Calls
jobs.desc: Which whale to chase, which deal to walk from, and how hard to push live in a person's read of the long game, not in pipeline math.

jobs.name: Proposal Final Sign-Off
jobs.desc: Nothing branded reaches a prospect without a human eye on the framing, the price, and the promise. Taste and accountability stay with Ahmed.

jobs.name: What to research next
jobs.desc: Deciding which accounts, verticals, and questions are worth the dig is a bet on where the business is going. AI answers the question; a human picks which questions matter.

jobs.name: Reading between the lines
jobs.desc: Knowing what a funding round or org reshuffle actually means for a deal is pattern-matching born of scars. The signal is automated; the so-what stays human.

jobs.name: Making the warm intro
jobs.desc: AI can find the shortest path into an account, but the actual ask trades on a personal relationship and reputation no agent can spend.

jobs.name: Trusting the call
jobs.desc: When verified research still contradicts a gut read, someone has to own the final judgment before money or reputation rides on it.

jobs.name: Brand Voice & Taste
jobs.desc: The final call on whether a piece sounds like Ahmed and earns the post. AI drafts to the pattern, but the line between on-brand and off stays a human judgment.

jobs.name: Content Strategy & Bets
jobs.desc: Which narratives to chase, which pillars to push this quarter, and which trends to ignore. The big directional bets that the whole engine then executes against.

jobs.name: Sponsor Relationships
jobs.desc: Negotiating deals and protecting trust with brand partners. The handshake, the pushback, and the long-term relationship can't be automated.

jobs.name: Final Publish Approval
jobs.desc: The go/no-go before anything reaches the audience under Ahmed's name. Reputation risk means a human signs off on what actually ships.

jobs.name: Scope & Trade-off Calls
jobs.desc: Deciding what to build, what to cut, and where the line of done sits is a commercial judgment call that shapes margin and the relationship. AI scaffolds and ships, but a human owns the scope.

jobs.name: Client Trust & Escalations
jobs.desc: When delivery slips or an incident hits a real client, the apology, the reset, and the hard conversation stay human. Trust through the quiet stretches is held by a person, not a status bot.

jobs.name: Architecture Decisions
jobs.desc: Choosing the stack, the integration shape, and the data model for a build is a one-way-door decision. AI executes the pattern, but the architecture taste stays with the operator.

jobs.name: Final Ship Approval
jobs.desc: The last look before a system goes live to a paying client is a reputation call. QA flags and verifies, but the sign-off that it's good enough to ship belongs to a human.

jobs.name: Offer & positioning
jobs.desc: The core promise · who you serve, the problem you solve, and how you frame the value. Sharp positioning is a judgment call that sets the ceiling on everything downstream.

jobs.name: Key-account relationships
jobs.desc: Trust, rapport, and the long game with the accounts that matter most. People buy from people · this stays human by design.

jobs.name: Brand-voice & final approvals
jobs.desc: The final read before anything ships · tone, taste, and the call on whether it sounds like you. AI drafts, you sign off.

jobs.name: Deal strategy on big accounts
jobs.desc: Reading the room, sequencing stakeholders, and shaping the deal on the accounts where the number is large enough to warrant a human owner.
